title: How much does it cost to run an electric fire?
description: An electric fire costs about 52p an hour at full 2kW output, and just pennies for the flame effect alone. Real UK figures, cost tables and ways to save.
blurb: What an electric fire really costs per hour and per winter, and why the flame effect alone is almost free.
lede: An electric fire promises the look of a real fire without the chimney, the sweeping or the ash, and millions of UK living rooms now have one set into a media wall or standing in an old fireplace. The worry is always the same: electric heating has a reputation for being expensive, so people hesitate to switch the thing on. The truth is more nuanced. The dancing flame effect costs almost nothing, while the heating element behind it costs the same as any other electric heater of the same wattage. Once you separate those two parts, working out the real cost of your evenings in front of it takes about thirty seconds.
short_answer: A typical electric fire has a 2kW maximum heat output, which costs about 52p an hour at the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh. On the common 1kW half-power setting that drops to about 26p an hour, and the flame effect on its own uses LED lighting that costs well under 1p an hour. A four-hour winter evening with the heat running at 1kW costs roughly £1.04.

sections.h2: What an electric fire actually is, and what it draws
sections.html:
Every electric fire, whether it is an inset fire, a wall-mounted media wall fire, a freestanding electric stove or a cheap fan-assisted suite fire, is really two separate appliances in one box:

  • The flame effect. Modern fires use LED lamps behind a spinning reflector or a mist generator to create the flames. This typically draws between 5 and 20 watts, about the same as a couple of LED light bulbs. Older fires with halogen or incandescent flame lamps can draw 60 watts or more, but anything sold in the last decade will be LED.
  • The heater. Almost all electric fires use a concealed fan heater or radiant element with two settings, usually 1kW and 2kW. A few compact models top out at 1.5kW. This is where virtually all of the running cost sits.

The two run independently. You can have the flames going all evening with no heat at all, which matters a lot for the cost, as the next section shows.

One thing you will not find on an electric fire is the familiar A to G rating you see on a fridge or washing machine. Space heaters are not labelled that way because every electric heater converts electricity into heat at essentially 100 per cent efficiency, so there is nothing to rank. Our guide to how energy labels work explains which appliances carry them and why. For an electric fire, the only number that matters is the wattage on the rating plate.

sections.h2: Cost per hour, per evening and per week
sections.html:
The sums are simple: kilowatts multiplied by hours multiplied by your unit rate. Here is what each setting on a typical electric fire costs at the current price cap, including a four-hour evening and a week of four-hour evenings.

SettingCost per hour4-hour evening7 evenings (28 hours)
Flame effect only (10W)0.3p1p7p
Low heat (750W)19.6p78p£5.48
Half power (1kW)26.1p£1.04£7.31
Medium (1.5kW)39.2p£1.57£10.97
Full power (2kW)52.2p£2.09£14.62

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem cap July to September 2026.

In practice your fire will usually cost less than the full-power row suggests, because most models have a thermostat. Once the room reaches the set temperature the element cycles off and on, so over an evening a fire set to 2kW might only draw power for half to two thirds of the time. In a small, well-insulated room a 2kW fire could average closer to 1kW, bringing that £2.09 evening down to nearer £1.

To see how those figures compare with your kettle, oven and tumble dryer, our hub on appliance running costs puts every household device on the same per-hour footing.

sections.h2: What a whole winter of use looks like
sections.html:
Hourly figures only mean much once you multiply them across a season, so here are three realistic usage patterns.

Ambience only

If you run just the flame effect for four hours every evening, all year round, a 10W LED effect uses about 14.6 kWh a year. That is £3.81 a year, or roughly 1p a night. The cosy look of an electric fire is, for all practical purposes, free.

Evening top-up heating

Running the heat at an average of 1kW for four hours a night through a 120-night winter uses 480 kWh, which costs about £125 for the season. This is the most common real-world pattern: the central heating does the background work and the fire adds comfort in the one room you are actually sitting in.

Main heat source for the room

Running 2kW for eight hours a day across a 150-day heating season uses 2,400 kWh, which costs about £627. At that point the electric fire has become serious heating, and it is worth asking whether it is the right tool, which the next section covers.

One small extra to be aware of: a fire with a remote control keeps a receiver circuit powered all the time, typically drawing around half a watt to one watt. That is only £1 to £2 a year, but if you like chasing those losses across the whole house, our guide to standby power shows where the real money hides.

sections.h2: Electric fire vs gas central heating and other heaters
sections.html:
An electric fire turns every unit of electricity into heat in the room, so in one sense it is 100 per cent efficient. The catch is the price of the fuel. At the current cap, electricity costs 26.11p per kWh while gas costs 7.33p. Even after allowing for a condensing boiler wasting 10 to 15 per cent of the gas up the flue, a kWh of heat from gas central heating costs roughly 8 to 9p, about a third of the same heat from an electric fire.

That does not automatically make the gas boiler cheaper to use, because the two systems heat different amounts of space. The fire heats one room; the boiler typically heats the whole house. If you only need one warm room for a few hours, a 1kW electric fire costing 26p an hour can beat firing up a boiler that pushes 8 to 10kW of heat into every radiator in the house. If you need several rooms warm, or warmth for most of the day, gas wins comfortably. We work through that trade-off properly in whether it is cheaper to heat one room or the whole house.

Against other electric heaters, an electric fire is neither cheap nor expensive: a 2kW fan heater, a 2kW oil-filled radiator and a 2kW electric fire all cost exactly the same 52p an hour to run flat out, because the wattage is the wattage. The differences are in how the heat is delivered and controlled, which our comparison of electric heater types covers in detail. What you are paying extra for with an electric fire is the look, not the heat.

sections.h2: How to keep the running cost down
sections.html:
Because the flame effect is nearly free and the element is the expensive part, every saving comes from running the element less or at a lower setting.

  • Use the flames without the heat. On mild evenings, or once the room is warm, switch the heat off and leave the effect running. You keep the atmosphere for a penny a night.
  • Prefer the 1kW setting. Half power for longer usually feels better than full power in bursts, and the thermostat will cycle less aggressively. A 2kW blast is only worth it to warm a cold room quickly.
  • Set the thermostat realistically. Every degree you ask for costs more. Aim the fire at keeping you comfortable on the sofa, not at heating the hallway through an open door.
  • Shut the door and close the curtains. A 1kW fire can hold a small sealed room at temperature; the same fire in a draughty open-plan space will run constantly and still feel feeble.
  • Use the timer. Most modern fires have one. Heating an empty room is the most expensive habit of all.
  • Do not use it for background heat. Hours of low-level electric heating to take the chill off a house costs roughly three times what gas would. Keep the fire for targeted, in-the-room comfort.

If your fire is an older model with halogen flame lamps, swapping to a modern LED unit will save a little on the effect, but do not buy a new fire to save electricity. The element costs the same either way, and the difference on the lamps is a few pounds a year.

An electric fire costs about 52p an hour at full 2kW power, 26p an hour at the usual 1kW setting, and well under 1p an hour for the flame effect alone, all at the July to September 2026 cap rate of 26.11p per kWh. Used the way most people actually use one, a few hours of supplementary heat in the living room on winter evenings, it adds something in the region of £100 to £150 to a year's electricity bill. Used as the main heat source for a room all season, it can cost £600 or more, and gas central heating or a heat pump would do the same job for roughly a third of the price per unit of heat. The smart approach is to treat the electric fire as what it is: a very cheap mood light attached to a perfectly ordinary, full-price electric heater. Enjoy the flames freely, and be deliberate about when you press the heat button.

faq.q: How much does an electric fire cost per hour?
faq.a: At the July to September 2026 price cap of 26.11p per kWh, a 2kW electric fire costs about 52p an hour at full power and about 26p an hour on its 1kW setting. With the thermostat cycling the element in a warm room, the real average is often lower.

faq.q: Does the flame effect use much electricity?
faq.a: No. Modern flame effects use LED lighting that draws around 5 to 20 watts, which costs well under 1p an hour. Running the flames for four hours every evening all year costs roughly £4, so you can leave the effect on without the heat for next to nothing.

faq.q: Is an electric fire cheaper than gas central heating?
faq.a: Per unit of heat, no. Heat from an electric fire costs about 26p per kWh against roughly 8 to 9p from a condensing gas boiler. But if you only need one room warm for a couple of hours, running a 1kW fire can still work out cheaper than heating the whole house with the boiler.

faq.q: Are electric fires expensive to run compared with other electric heaters?
faq.a: No, they cost exactly the same as any other electric heater of the same wattage. A 2kW electric fire, a 2kW fan heater and a 2kW oil-filled radiator all cost about 52p an hour flat out. You pay extra up front for the flame effect, not in running cost.

faq.q: Does an electric fire use power when it is switched off?
faq.a: Models with a remote control keep a small receiver powered, typically drawing around 0.5 to 1 watt on standby. That works out at £1 to £2 a year. Switching the fire off at the wall over summer removes even that.
